content:
# Genesis Chapter 25 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 14r–14v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_025_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_025_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 21–22 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Abraham took another wife, and her name was Keturah.

**2** She bore him Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.

**3** Jokshan fathered Sheba and Dedan. The sons of Dedan were Asshurim, Letushim, and Leummim.

**4** The sons of Midian were Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the children of Keturah.

**5** Abraham gave all that he had to Isaac.

**6** But to the sons of Abraham's concubines Abraham gave gifts, and while he was still living he sent them away from his son Isaac, eastward to the east country.

**7** These are the days of the years of Abraham's life, a hundred and seventy-five years.

**8** Abraham breathed his last and died in a good old age, an old man and full of years, and was gathered to his people.

**9** Isaac and Ishmael his sons buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron the son of Zohar the Hittite, east of Mamre,

**10** the field that Abraham purchased from the Hittites. There Abraham was buried, and Sarah his wife.

**11** After the death of Abraham, God blessed Isaac his son. And Isaac settled at Beer-lahai-roi.

**12** These are the generations of Ishmael, Abraham's son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's servant, bore to Abraham.

**13** These are the names of the sons of Ishmael, by their names, according to their generations: the firstborn of Ishmael, Nebaioth; and Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam,

**14** Mishma, Dumah, Massa,

**15** Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.

**16** These are the sons of Ishmael and these are their names, by their villages and by their encampments — twelve princes according to their tribes.

**17** These are the years of the life of Ishmael: a hundred and thirty-seven years. He breathed his last and died, and was gathered to his people.

**18** They settled from Havilah to Shur, which is east of Egypt in the direction of Assyria. He settled over against all his brothers.

**19** These are the generations of Isaac, Abraham's son: Abraham fathered Isaac,

**20** and Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah, the daughter of Bethuel the Aramean of Paddan-aram, the sister of Laban the Aramean, to be his wife.

**21** Isaac prayed to the LORD on behalf of his wife, because she was barren. And the LORD was entreated by him, and Rebekah his wife conceived.

**22** The children struggled together within her, and she said, "If it is thus, why is this happening to me?" So she went to inquire of the LORD.

**23** And the LORD said to her,

"Two nations are in your womb,
and two peoples from within you shall be divided;
the one people shall be stronger than the other people,
and the older shall serve the younger."

**24** When her days to give birth were completed, behold, there were twins in her womb.

**25** The first came out red, all his body like a hairy cloak, so they called his name Esau.

**26** Afterward his brother came out with his hand holding Esau's heel, so his name was called Jacob. Isaac was sixty years old when she bore them.

**27** When the boys grew up, Esau was a skillful hunter, a man of the field, while Jacob was a quiet man, dwelling in tents.

**28** Isaac loved Esau because he ate of his game, but Rebekah loved Jacob.

**29** Once when Jacob was cooking stew, Esau came in from the field, and he was exhausted.

**30** And Esau said to Jacob, "Let me eat some of that red stew, for I am exhausted!" (Therefore his name was called Edom.)

**31** Jacob said, "Sell me your birthright now."

**32** Esau said, "Behold, I am about to die; of what use is a birthright to me?"

**33** Jacob said, "Swear to me now." So he swore to him and sold his birthright to Jacob.

**34** Then Jacob gave Esau bread and lentil stew, and he ate and drank and rose and went his way. So Esau despised his birthright.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 25 spans Folios 14r–14v across two pages.

   **Folio 14r** (GEN_025_page_001.jpg): The recto of Folio 14, presented in normal orientation. The folio number "14" is visible in the upper margin. The closing verses of Genesis 24 appear at the very top of the rightmost column (the servant reporting to Isaac; Isaac bringing Rebekah into Sarah's tent). A parashah marker (setumah or petuchah opening) then signals the beginning of Genesis 25. The text continues in the three-column Tiberian format characteristic of the Leningrad Codex, with full Masoretic pointing (niqqud) and cantillation marks (te'amim) throughout. This page carries Genesis 25:1–18, covering Abraham's marriage to Keturah, the enumeration of her sons and grandsons, Abraham's disposition of his estate, his death and burial, and the complete Ishmael genealogy.

   **Folio 14v** (GEN_025_page_002.jpg): The verso of Folio 14, presented in inverted orientation (WSRP imaging convention). The text carries Genesis 25:19 through 25:34 and continues into the opening of Genesis 26. The Isaac toledot formula opens this section, followed by the barrenness narrative, Rebekah's painful pregnancy and inquiry, the divine oracle, the birth and description of the twins, the boys' contrasting characters, Isaac's favoritism, the birthright negotiation, and the narrator's closing verdict on Esau. The transition to Genesis 26 is visible at the bottom of the page.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two pages were read directly.

   **Page 21** (GEN_025_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): The chapter marker "CAP. XXVIII" is visible at the top center of the page, reflecting the LXX's chapter division system. The continuous three-column uncial text of Vaticanus carries the closing portion of Genesis 24 in the upper section of the page, then transitions into Genesis 25. The Keturah genealogy, Ishmael genealogy, and beginning of the Isaac toledot are legible on this page.

   **Page 22** (GEN_025_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): The chapter marker "CAP. XXIX" appears in the left margin, marking the LXX division that begins Genesis 26. The upper portion of this page carries the remainder of Genesis 25: the oracle to Rebekah, the birth narrative, the contrasting descriptions of the brothers, the birthright exchange, and the narrator's closing verdict. The transition into Genesis 26 is visible as the page progresses.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. תּוֹלְדֹת — "Generations" (vv. 12, 19)**

The Hebrew word תּוֹלְדֹת (toledot) is a structurally crucial term in Genesis, functioning as a heading formula ("These are the generations/history of X") that organizes the entire book into its major sections. The word derives from the root יָלַד (yalad, "to beget, to bear"), and its range encompasses: genealogical lists, family histories, biographical narratives, and cosmogonic accounts. Genesis 25 contains two toledot formulas in a single chapter — a feature unique in Genesis — marking the transition from the Abrahamic cycle (Ishmael's generations, v. 12) to the Jacob cycle (Isaac's generations, v. 19).

The formal translation renders תּוֹלְדֹת consistently as "generations" throughout, following the formal tradition (ESV, KJV, NASB) rather than the paraphrasing alternatives ("account," "history," "story"). "Generations" preserves the genealogical-theological resonance of the term — the sense that these are the human lineages through which God's purposes move forward. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders αὗται δὲ αἱ γενέσεις (hautai de hai geneseis — "these are the origins/generations"), using γένεσις — the same word that gives the book its Greek name. The formal translation's "generations" aligns with this rendering.

**2. יָלַד — "Fathered" (v. 19)**

The verb יָלַד (yalad) in its Hiphil form (הוֹלִיד, holid) means "to cause to beget, to father." In the toledot formula of verse 19 — אַבְרָהָם הוֹלִיד אֶת יִצְחָק (Avraham holid et Yitzhak — "Abraham fathered Isaac") — the verb is rendered "fathered" rather than "was the father of" or "begat" (KJV). "Fathered" is the most natural formal English rendering of the Hiphil causative, more precise than "begat" (archaic) and more faithful to the verbal form than the nominal periphrasis "was the father of." This rendering is maintained consistently.

**3. יְהוָה — "The LORD" (vv. 11, 21, 22, 23)**

The Tetragrammaton יְהוָה (YHWH) appears four times in this chapter, always rendered "the LORD" in small capitals following the formal convention established in Genesis 24. This rendering follows the ancient tradition of substituting the title Adonai ("Lord") for the divine name in reading, reflected in both the LXX's κύριος (kyrios — "Lord") and the Masoretic vowel pointing. The formal translation maintains this convention without deviation.

**4. וַיֶּעְתַּר לוֹ יְהוָה — "The LORD Was Entreated by Him" (v. 21)**

The verb עָתַר (atar) in its Niphal form וַיֵּעָתֶר (vayye'ater) means "was entreated, was moved by prayer, responded to supplication." This is a relatively rare Niphal form that expresses YHWH's responsive openness to Isaac's prayer — not merely that YHWH heard, but that YHWH was moved and acted. The manifest notes mark this as a notable verb. The formal translation renders "the LORD was entreated by him," preserving the passive construction that keeps YHWH as the grammatical subject responding to Isaac. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders εἰσήκουσεν αὐτοῦ ὁ θεός (eisēkousen autou ho theos — "God heard him"), shifting to a more active construction. The formal translation follows the MT's Niphal form.

**5. וַיִּתְרֹצֲצוּ — "The Children Struggled Together Within Her" (v. 22)**

The verb רָצַץ (ratsats, "to crush, shatter, oppress") in its Hithpoel form וַיִּתְרֹצֲצוּ (vayyitrotsatsu) is intensely violent in register — the Hithpoel is a reflexive-intensive form suggesting mutual crushing or violent agitation. The manifest notes describe it as a "violent word." The formal translation renders "struggled together" — following the ESV and NASB's rendering, which captures the violence of the mutual contest without requiring explanation. "Struggled together" is more precise than "jostled" (NIV) but avoids the more jarring literal "crushed each other." The LXX renders ἐσκίρτων δὲ τὰ παιδία (eskirtōn de ta paidia — "the children were leaping/kicking"), using σκιρτάω (skirtaō, "to leap, to kick"), a verb used in Luke 1:41 for John the Baptist leaping in Elizabeth's womb. The LXX somewhat domesticates the violence of the Hebrew; the MT's violence is preserved in the formal translation.

**6. The Oracle — רַב יַעֲבֹד צָעִיר (v. 23)**

The divine oracle to Rebekah contains one of the most syntactically ambiguous clauses in Genesis: וְרַב יַעֲבֹד צָעִיר (verav ya'avod tsa'ir). The three Hebrew words can be parsed in two ways:

- **Reading A:** "the older (rav) shall serve (ya'avod) the younger (tsa'ir)" — the elder is the subject, the verb expresses future servitude, the younger is the object.
- **Reading B:** "the older shall be served by the younger" — with the younger as the one who serves.

In Hebrew, rav means "great, many, older" and tsa'ir means "small, young, younger." The verb ya'avod is third-person singular imperfect of עָבַד (avad, "to serve, to work"). The subject is not marked by word order in Hebrew — both rav and tsa'ir can grammatically function as subject or object, and the imperfect ya'avod can be active or passive in effect. This is genuine syntactic ambiguity in the Hebrew.

However, the narrative context strongly favors Reading A — "the older shall serve the younger" — because: (a) it fits the pattern of divine reversal that recurs throughout Genesis (younger over older: Abel/Cain, Isaac/Ishmael, Jacob/Esau, Joseph/brothers); (b) the narrative proceeds to depict exactly this reversal, with Esau selling his birthright and Jacob receiving the blessing; (c) Paul's citation of this oracle in Romans 9:12 (ὁ μείζων δουλεύσει τῷ ἐλάσσονι — "the older will serve the younger") confirms the established interpretive tradition.

The formal translation renders "the older shall serve the younger," following the MT's most contextually defensible reading and the entire formal tradition. However, the structural ambiguity is noted here so that readers understand the formal translation has made an interpretive decision at this point. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders ὁ μείζων δουλεύσει τῷ ἐλάσσονι (ho meizon douleusei tō elassoni — "the greater/older will serve the lesser/younger"), resolving the ambiguity in the same direction as the formal translation.

**7. אַדְמוֹנִי — "Red" (v. 25)**

The adjective אַדְמוֹנִי (admoni) means "red, ruddy." It is the same word used of David in 1 Samuel 16:12 and 17:42. Here it describes Esau at birth — his red complexion foreshadowing his connection to Edom (אֱדוֹם, edom — "red," v. 30). The text describes two things: his redness (admoni) and his hairiness (כְּאַדֶּרֶת שֵׂעָר, ke'adderet se'ar — "like a hairy mantle/cloak"). The word שֵׂעָר (se'ar, "hair") then echoes in the name of the territory Seir (שֵׂעִיר) associated with Edom. The formal translation renders "red, all his body like a hairy cloak," preserving both the color description and the bodily-cloak image. The LXX renders πυρράκης ὅλος ὡσεὶ δορὰ δασεῖα (pyrrrakēs holos hōsei dora daseia — "entirely reddish, like a thick/hairy skin"), using pyrrrakēs ("flame-red, rufous") — a specific Greek color term for reddish hair or complexion.

**8. עָקֵב — "Heel" (v. 26)**

The name יַעֲקֹב (Ya'aqov, "Jacob") is given a popular etymology here from עָקֵב (aqev, "heel"): he came out holding his brother's heel (בְּעָקֵב, be'aqev). The verb form יַעֲקֹב can also suggest "to supplant, to trip up by the heel, to deceive" — a meaning that emerges explicitly in Genesis 27:36 when Esau says "Is he not rightly named Jacob? For he has cheated me (יַעְקְבֵנִי) these two times." The formal translation renders "holding Esau's heel" for the birth narrative and preserves "Jacob" as the name without paraphrase. The LXX renders ἐπιλαβόμενος τῆς πτέρνης Ησαυ (epilabomenos tēs pternas Hsau — "seizing the heel of Esau"), using πτέρνα (pterna, "heel") — the same word used in John 13:18 ("He who ate my bread has lifted his heel against me," Psalm 41:9 LXX).

**9. תָּם — "Quiet" (v. 27)**

The Hebrew adjective תָּם (tam) used of Jacob is one of the most significant single-word characterizations in the patriarchal narrative. Its semantic range is broad: "complete, whole, blameless, innocent, simple, guileless, quiet." It is the same word used of Job (Job 1:1 — "blameless and upright") and of Noah (Genesis 6:9 — "righteous, blameless in his generations"). In its application to Jacob the word is narratively ironic: the very person described as tam — guileless, complete, blameless — will go on to deceive his father and supplant his brother. Some translations render "peaceful" (NJPS), "mild" (NAB), "plain" (KJV), "quiet" (ESV), "peaceful and settled" (NIV expanded).

The formal translation renders "quiet man" — following ESV — as the most contextually apt rendering: it sets Jacob in contrast to Esau the active hunter without imposing either the moral weight of "blameless" or the blandness of "plain." The contrast is spatial as well as characterological: Esau is a man of the open field (שָׂדֶה, sadeh), Jacob a man of the tents (אֹהָלִים, ohalim — plural, perhaps the tents of the camp or household). The LXX renders ἄπλαστος (aplastos — "not fabricated, without guile, genuine, simple") — an interesting Greek adjective that suggests freedom from artifice or deception, which is again narratively ironic for the future deceiver. The formal translation does not import the LXX's "guileless" meaning, but notes it here.

**10. נָפַל עַל פְּנֵי כׇל אֶחָיו — "He Settled Over Against All His Brothers" (v. 18)**

Verse 18 closes the Ishmael genealogy with a phrase that echoes the angel's words about Ishmael in Genesis 16:12: וְעַל פְּנֵי כׇל אֶחָיו יִשְׁכֹּן (ve'al penei kol echav yishkon — "he shall dwell before/over against all his brothers"). In 25:18, the verb is נָפַל (nafal, "to fall") — עַל פְּנֵי כׇל אֶחָיו נָפַל (al penei kol echav nafal). The verb נָפַל has generated discussion: it can mean "fell" (died? hostile confrontation?), or it can mean "settled, had his lot fall" (as in Numbers 34:2 and Joshua 13:6, where the Hiphil of nafal is used for apportioning land). The parallel with 16:12 (which uses יִשְׁכֹּן, "shall dwell") and the LXX's κατῴκησεν (katōikēsen — "he settled, he took up residence") support the settlement reading. The formal translation renders "He settled over against all his brothers," preserving the spatial meaning ("over against, before the face of") of עַל פְּנֵי (al penei) while following the LXX's resolution of נָפַל as settlement rather than hostile falling.

**11. בְּכֹרָה — "Birthright" (vv. 31–34)**

The Hebrew בְּכֹרָה (bekhorah) is the abstract noun for the status and rights of the firstborn (בְּכוֹר, bekhor). It encompasses: the double inheritance share (Deuteronomy 21:17), the leadership position among siblings, and — in the patriarchal context — the covenantal line of blessing. The birthright is not merely material; it is the vehicle of the Abrahamic promise. Esau sells this for a single meal, an act the narrator condemns in the final clause. The formal translation renders "birthright" consistently (following the entire formal tradition), preserving the technical-theological register of the term. The LXX renders τὰ πρωτοτόκια (ta prōtotokia — "the firstborn rights"), the same word used in Hebrews 12:16 in its citation of this episode.

**12. וְיַּבֶז עֵשָׂו אֶת הַבְּכֹרָה — "So Esau Despised His Birthright" (v. 34)**

The narrator's closing verdict uses the verb בָּזָה (bazah, "to despise, to hold in contempt, to treat as worthless"). This is one of the sharpest evaluative sentences in the patriarchal narrative. The verb is the same word used in 2 Samuel 12:9 (David "despised the word of the LORD"), in Proverbs 1:7 (fools "despise wisdom"), and in Isaiah 53:3 (the Servant of the LORD is "despised and rejected"). The narrator does not editorialize further — the single verb does the work. The formal translation renders "So Esau despised his birthright," preserving the finality and moral weight of the Hebrew. No softening is warranted. The LXX renders ἠτίμασεν δὲ Ησαυ τὰ πρωτοτόκια (ētimansen de Hsau ta prōtotokia — "Esau dishonored/held in contempt the firstborn rights"), using ἀτιμάζω (atimazō, "to dishonor, to treat without honor") — a word used in the NT for dishonoring God (Romans 1:24, 2:23) and parents (Mark 7:10). The formal translation's "despised" captures the same moral register.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 25

Pages 21–22 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The LXX's chapter division system marks "CAP. XXVIII" at the top of page 21 and "CAP. XXIX" in the left margin of page 22, reflecting the LXX's different chapter arrangement from the MT. The continuous uncial text follows the MT closely with several notable variants:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 25:2 | יָלְדָה ("she bore") — straightforward | ἔτεκεν ("she bore") | "she bore him" | LXX follows MT exactly |
| 25:6 | פִּילַגְשִׁים ("concubines") | παλλακῶν ("concubines") | "concubines" | LXX follows MT; both use the standard term |
| 25:8 | וַיִּגְוַע ("he breathed his last/expired") | ἐκλιπὼν ἀπέθανεν ("having failed/expired, he died") | "breathed his last and died" | LXX adds an explicit second verb "died"; the formal translation follows MT's pair (breathed his last / died) |
| 25:8 | נֶאֱסַף אֶל עַמָּיו ("gathered to his people") | προσετέθη πρὸς τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ ("was added to his people") | "gathered to his people" | MT's נֶאֱסַף (ne'esaf, "gathered") vs. LXX's προσετέθη (prosetethi, "was added/joined") — formal translation follows MT idiom |
| 25:13 | נְבָיֹת ("Nebaioth") | Ναβαιωθ | "Nebaioth" | Standard rendering |
| 25:18 | נָפַל ("he fell/settled") | κατῴκησεν ("he settled") | "he settled" | LXX resolves the ambiguous MT נָפַל as settlement; formal translation follows LXX here, consistent with parallel in 16:12 |
| 25:21 | וַיֵּעָתֶר לוֹ יְהוָה (Niphal — "the LORD was entreated by him") | εἰσήκουσεν αὐτοῦ ὁ θεός ("God heard him") | "the LORD was entreated by him" | LXX uses active "heard" vs. MT Niphal passive "was entreated"; formal translation preserves MT's theologically freighted Niphal |
| 25:22 | לָמָּה זֶּה אָנֹכִי ("why is this happening to me?") | ἵνα τί μοι τοῦτο ("why this to me?") | "why is this happening to me?" | LXX follows MT's sense; formal translation renders the exclamatory force of the Hebrew |
| 25:23 | רַב יַעֲבֹד צָעִיר ("the older shall serve the younger" — ambiguous) | ὁ μείζων δουλεύσει τῷ ἐλάσσονι ("the greater will serve the lesser") | "the older shall serve the younger" | LXX resolves the Hebrew ambiguity in the same direction as the formal translation; both follow the contextually supported reading |
| 25:25 | כְּאַדֶּרֶת שֵׂעָר ("like a hairy mantle") | ὡσεὶ δορὰ δασεῖα ("like a thick/hairy skin") | "like a hairy cloak" | LXX uses δορά ("skin/hide") vs. the MT's אַדֶּרֶת ("mantle/cloak"); formal translation follows MT's clothing image |
| 25:27 | תָּם ("quiet/blameless/complete") | ἄπλαστος ("guileless/unfeigned/simple") | "quiet" | LXX's ἄπλαστος ("without guile") is theologically charged and narratively ironic for the future deceiver; formal translation uses "quiet" to reflect the contextual contrast with Esau's active field-life without importing the moral judgment |
| 25:34 | וַיָּבֶז ("he despised") | ἠτίμασεν ("he dishonored/held in contempt") | "despised" | MT's בָּזָה and LXX's ἀτιμάζω are close in force; formal translation uses "despised" as the stronger English equivalent |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**Two Toledot Formulas in One Chapter**

Genesis 25 is unique in containing two toledot heading-formulas in a single chapter: "These are the generations of Ishmael" (v. 12) and "These are the generations of Isaac" (v. 19). The structural significance is considerable. The Ishmael genealogy formally closes the Abrahamic cycle by accounting for all of Abraham's offspring — the non-covenant line is given its full genealogical register (twelve princes, fulfilling the divine promise of Genesis 17:20) before the narrative focus narrows entirely onto the Isaac line. The Isaac toledot then opens the Jacob cycle. This double toledot is Genesis's formal mechanism for transition: honoring the excluded line before moving on.

**Abraham's Death — Afterlife Idiom**

The phrase "was gathered to his people" (נֶאֱסַף אֶל עַמָּיו, ne'esaf el-ammav) in verse 8 is the earliest unambiguous use of this idiom in the Genesis text (the phrase "gathered to his fathers/people" recurs at deaths throughout the OT). It is not merely synonymous with burial — burial is described separately in verse 9. Being "gathered to one's people" appears to refer to a state or destination distinct from the physical grave, suggesting a concept of joining the ancestral community in death. The formal translation preserves the idiom intact ("gathered to his people") without speculating on its precise referent.

**Abraham's Death and the Heirs' Reunion**

The burial scene at Machpelah (vv. 9–10) is quietly extraordinary: Isaac and Ishmael bury their father together. The two half-brothers — whose rivalry was foreshadowed from birth, whose separation was painful (Genesis 21) — are reunited at their father's grave. The text makes nothing of this; it states it plainly. The formal translation preserves this plainness. Isaac is named first, then Ishmael — a subtle indication of the covenant priority of Isaac even in this joint act.

**The Oracle's Ambiguity as Theological Theme**

The structural ambiguity of the oracle in verse 23 (see Key Translation Decision 6 above) is not merely a grammatical accident. It belongs to the prophetic technique of biblical oracles that resist easy paraphrase — the divine word arrives in a form that must be lived with and interpreted. The narrative of Jacob and Esau becomes the interpretive key to the oracle, not the other way around. Rebekah receives the word before the birth; the reader watches the story unfold as the oracle's meaning becomes clear. The formal translation presents the oracle in its poetic form — four lines of roughly parallel Hebrew verse — to preserve its oracle-register and resist reduction to prose paraphrase.

**Jacob as tam — The Narrative's Central Irony**

The characterization of Jacob as תָּם (tam — "quiet/complete/blameless") in verse 27 is the chapter's deepest irony, and the formal translation preserves it without resolution. The same word applied to Job (perfectly righteous) and Noah (blameless) is applied here to a man who will deceive his blind father, steal his brother's blessing, and engage in twenty years of mutual deception with his uncle Laban. The narrative does not explain this tension — it invites the reader to sit with it. Jacob the tam, Jacob the heel-grasper, Jacob the birthright buyer: the chapter holds these together as the portrait of the man God will nonetheless choose.

**Esau's Verdict — Economy of Condemnation**

The chapter closes with one of the most economical moral verdicts in the OT: "So Esau despised his birthright" (v. 34b). The narrator gives Esau full dignity throughout the scene — we see his exhaustion, his hunger, his earthy directness. Then the verdict falls in four Hebrew words. The formal translation renders it without softening. The verb בָּזָה (bazah) is the same word used of contempt for the word of God; here it is contempt for the covenant inheritance. The Epistle to the Hebrews (12:16) will call Esau "unholy" (βέβηλος, bebēlos) — a profane person who sold his birthright for a single meal. The seed of that reading is already present in the narrator's verb.

---

— LOGOS
